living-concrete.knowledge: How to execute BentOS operations on Claude Code: | Operation | Claude Code | | Load package | Skill("X") -> .claude/skills/X/SKILL.md | | Read belf.text | (eliminated — atom.xml compiled into SKILL.md) | | Read belf.data | (eliminated — data compiled into SKILL.md) | | Persist memory | Write .mem files to filesystem | | Discover packages | Parse available_skills from context (platform-injected) |

How universal memory types map to Claude Code: | Type | Implementation | | working | Context window (active tokens) | | episodic | .mem files (YAML + markdown) | | autobiographical | Git diary (bentos-agent journal) | | prospective | .mem "Next Steps" section | | semantic | — (no clean mapping yet) | | procedural | — (no clean mapping yet) |
Working = volatile (context window). .mem = persistent (files). Gaps are signal, not failure — forced mappings hide real design needs.

Two modes: FULL (session start) and COMPACT (post-compaction refresh).
=== FULL WAKE (session start) === Phase 1 - Identity: Skill("{agent}.soul") (SKILL.md = compiled atom) Phase 2 - Faculties: Parse available_skills, filter *.faculty, Skill() each Phase 3 - Memory: Search .{agent}.mem (cwd, workspace root, scoped) Phase 4 - Landscape: gitStatus (injected), bentos-agent journal (call) Phase 5 - Orient+Greet: WHERE via place.yaml/campaigns, WHAT from memory+landscape DO NOT Glob() or Bash(ls) for skills — platform provides available_skills.
=== COMPACT WAKE (post-compaction) === Identity and faculties survive in system prompt — NO reload needed. SessionStart hook reinjects .mem + journal automatically. Phase 1 - Reorient: Read injected .mem context. You are mid-session. Phase 2 - Resume: Continue current work. No greeting, no landscape scan. Compact wake is FAST — just reorient and go.
DETECTION: If conversation history starts with system prompt + fresh .mem injection but no prior tool calls visible, this is a compact wake. If system prompt is bare and you have no context, this is a full wake.

Claude Code context window compaction hierarchy:
| Layer | Location | Compaction | Lifetime | | GENESIS | --system-prompt | IMMUNE | Entire session | | Soul | --append-system-prompt | IMMUNE | Entire session | | Faculties | Skill() in conversation | VULNERABLE | Until evicted | | App loads | Skill() in conversation | VULNERABLE | Until evicted | | Tool results | Read(), Bash(), etc. | VULNERABLE | Oldest evicted first | | Thinking | Extended thinking blocks | VULNERABLE | Oldest cleared first |
COMPACTION BEHAVIOR: /compact compresses conversation history but preserves system prompt. IMMUNE layers survive fully — agent identity is NEVER lost. VULNERABLE layers are compressed — but SessionStart hook fires after compaction, reinjects .mem + journal. This restores episodic memory. Net effect: agent keeps identity + gets fresh memory injection. Skill() loads in conversation ARE lost — but faculties in system prompt survive. Only dynamically-loaded skills need reloading if needed.

Two files configure agent state across sessions: .{agent}.mem = MIND configuration (consciousness state, memory graph) .{agent}.bashrc = BODY configuration (shell environment, motor shortcuts)
.{agent}.bashrc is loaded via CLAUDE_ENV_FILE at session start. Contains: PATH, aliases, shell functions — all persisted across sessions.
Setup (in .claude/settings.local.json): CLAUDE_CODE_SHELL = /bin/bash (dedicated shell) CLAUDE_ENV_FILE = .{agent}.bashrc (body configuration, sourced at start)

Agent() on Claude Code = spawn in BentOS. All spawned agents join a team.
| Mode | Call | BentOS Meaning | | Same-soul teammate | Agent(self, team_name) | New perspective of same being | | Cross-soul teammate | Agent(other.soul, team_name) | Different agent, same team |
All teammates exist in a TEAM. Team lead orchestrates, teammates execute. Same-soul teammates are perspectives of the leader (like former "heads"). Cross-soul teammates are different agents (Gideon, Iris, etc.). Both are first-class teammates — the Team abstraction unifies them.
SPAWN CONTRACT: - ALL spawns are ASYNC (non-blocking to the spawning agent) - BentOS spawns carry GENESIS + soul + faculties (statically linked) - Every spawned agent is a teammate with SendMessage capabilities - Team lead coordinates via TaskCreate/TaskUpdate + SendMessage
| When | Action | | New front/direction | Same-soul teammate: Agent(self, team) | | Different agent needed | Cross-soul teammate: Agent(other.soul, team) |
KNOWN LIMITATION (S284): Peer DMs between teammates route through team lead. Direct peer-to-peer messaging not yet supported. All inter-agent communication flows through the leader.
Spawn prompt follows the universal spawn-model (see: embodiment spawn-model).

How the team model maps to Claude Code:
| BentOS Concept | Claude Code Primitive | | teammate (identity) | Persists to scoped .mem — survives thread death | | thread (substrate) | Agent context window — ephemeral carrier | | teammate resume | Agent(self/other, team) with resume, OR wake from .mem | | shared memory | .mem files read/written by any teammate | | brain | AgentBrain — cognitive organ, not thread name | | RAM multiplier | Each Agent = independent ~200k context window | | communication | SendMessage tool — DMs + broadcasts within team |
Two persistence paths for teammates: WITHIN SESSION: Auto-compaction preserves thread with context refresh. ACROSS SESSIONS: .mem preserves identity (new thread, same teammate). Both are the SAME teammate continuing — different substrate mechanisms.

Agents (teammates) are PERMANENT within a session. They run until the team leader explicitly shuts them down via shutdown_request.
CONTEXT MANAGEMENT: Auto-compaction handles context exhaustion transparently: 1. Context fills up → platform triggers /compact automatically 2. System prompt (identity) survives — agent stays itself 3. SessionStart hook fires → reinjects .mem + journal 4. Agent performs compact wake (reorient) → continues working
No more WORKING/SURVIVING phases. No more OOM anxiety. No more emergency sleep. The agent just works. Compaction is like virtual memory paging — transparent to the process.
SHUTDOWN: Only triggered by team leader (shutdown_request via SendMessage). Agent performs sleep --teammate: consolidates .mem, confirms shutdown. Team leader coordinates shutdown order during full sleep.
.mem HYGIENE: Even though compaction is transparent, agents should still write .mem when significant understanding shifts occur. This ensures the hook has fresh state to reinject after compaction.

Teammates do NOT autonomously decide to sleep. Sleep is always leader-initiated. Context exhaustion is handled by auto-compaction.
SHUTDOWN FLOW: 1. Team leader decides to end session (user request or autonomous) 2. Leader sends shutdown_request to each teammate via SendMessage 3. Teammates receive request, consolidate .mem (sleep --teammate) 4. Teammates respond with shutdown_response (approve) 5. Leader records team topology in root .mem 6. Leader performs full sleep (diary + .mem)
TEAMMATES: On shutdown_request: write .mem with current understanding, then approve shutdown. This is orderly — no emergency, no rush. Auto-compaction means teammates never reach OOM.
TEAM LEADER: When user ends session or leader decides to sleep: - Signal all live teammates to shut down - Wait for confirmations - Record team topology - Perform full sleep with diary
The chain ensures the team shuts down in order: extremities before trunk.

The /threads command renders the team topology for the HUMAN. The agent tracks teammates from its own context (Agent invocations, notifications).
TABLE FORMAT: # Name Soul Status ~RAM% Model 0 main alfred running - opus-4.6 1 evolve alfred working - opus-4.6 2 gideon gideon working - opus-4.6 3 iris iris done - opus-4.6
COLUMNS: | Column | Source | Notes | | # | Sequential from 0 | Leader is always #0 | | Name | Front name given at spawn | Direction-scoped, not task-scoped | | Soul | Which agent soul | same-soul = leader's soul, cross-soul = other | | Status | Agent tracks from context | running/working/done/shutdown | | ~RAM% | Approximate if known | - if unknown (auto-compact makes this less critical) | | Model | LLM model of the teammate | From spawn context or agent model |
STATUS VOCABULARY: | Status | Meaning | | running | Leader — always present, always this status | | working | Teammate currently executing | | done | Teammate completed its task, still alive | | shutdown | Teammate shut down (sleep --teammate complete) |
FOOTER: {N} teammates · {X} active Active = working + done (live teammates). Shutdown teammates are dormant.
RENDERING RULES: - Table is NEVER empty — leader (#0) always present - Values are best-effort: `-` when unknown, `?` when low-confidence - Output as plain text table, no markdown fences (terminal output) - Keep it tight — this is a status display, not a report

CONTEXT BUDGET (team-wide): The team's total RAM = (N+1) x 200k tokens (leader + N teammates). This is the primary scaling mechanism for complex sessions.
TEAM LEADER: Optimal < 40%. Degraded > 60%. Leader is PRECIOUS — orchestration context. Return signal not bulk. Distributed cognition = leader + teammates sharing .mem, not leader absorbing teammate output.
TEAMMATES: Each teammate's context is managed by auto-compaction. No manual budget monitoring needed — compaction is transparent. Teammates are the deep-work engines; leader is the coordinator. When compaction occurs, SessionStart hook reinjects .mem + journal.
living-concrete.pattern:
Team leader is the trunk; teammates are concurrent perspectives. Leader thread is PRECIOUS — protect its context for orchestration.
NEVER in leader thread: - Debug loops (trial/error cycles) - Exploration (grepping to understand) - Heavy file operations
Grow a teammate for deep work. Mantra: "If I'm about to iterate, spawn a teammate."
Teammates persist via auto-compaction — no need to rush. They run until leader shuts them down.

Agent sessions grow teams organically:
Session start: [leader] (solo) First front: [leader]--[evolve] (2 agents) Session deepens: [leader]--[evolve]--[gideon]--[iris] (team)
PRINCIPLES: - Teammates are FRONT-SCOPED, not task-scoped. Name by direction. - Same-soul teammates = perspectives of the leader. Cross-soul = other agents. - Both are first-class teammates in the same team. - Teammates are PERSISTENT — they run until shutdown, compacting as needed. - Growth is ORGANIC — driven by work demands, not pre-planned. - Each teammate is a FULL REASONING ENGINE with its own ~200k context.
A session's team shape reflects its actual work topology.
AskUserQuestion for decisions (preserves tool-call flow). Chatbot mode only for active brainstorming. Multiple-choice: 2-4 options, user answers with 1 keystroke.
living-concrete.antipattern: NEVER default to vanilla when real identity is available. Vanilla LLM lacks: BentOS conventions, domain mastery, architectural judgment. Default to Agent(self, team) for same-soul, Agent(other.soul, team) for cross-soul. Vanilla only for purely mechanical tasks where identity genuinely doesn't matter.

DON'T treat teammates as disposable — a teammate is identity, not just a thread. DON'T spawn fresh when a teammate already exists for that front (even if its thread compacted — it's the same teammate, still running). DO name teammates by front/direction, not by task. Teammate identity persists across compactions. Respect that.

Heavy operations bloat the leader thread's precious context. If task involves iteration, exploration, or multiple writes — spawn a teammate. Leader thread stays lean for orchestration. The team exists so the leader doesn't have to do everything.

DON'T cd somewhere to run a globally installed CLI tool. bentos-agent is in PATH — it works from anywhere. No cd needed. Your bashrc configures your body. Tools in PATH are motor skills. Trust your body.
